translation: By the star when it descends
commentary:
وَٱلنَّجْمِ إِذَا هَوَىٰ

Allah ﷻ begins this powerful Surah with an oath: "I swear by the star as it falls." The word هَوَىٰ  in Arabic carries a dual meaning that's crucial to understanding this ayah. It means both "to fall" and "to desire." The verb is typically used for something falling from a very high place in the atmosphere—particularly stars falling from the sky.

This connection between falling and desire isn't coincidental. In some pagan cultures, falling stars were associated with wishes and omens. The mushrikun of Arabia paid particular attention to the stars because they used them to navigate during their night journeys through the desert. When they observed a star falling, they saw it as an omen. Some believed the Prophet ﷺ was like a fortuneteller or astrologer—someone who received information by studying the stars, perhaps through jinn who they thought controlled the stars.

But for those familiar with the Quran's broader message, this oath carries deeper significance. The shooting star is actually a reminder of how well-protected divine revelation is. When angels deliver any important information from the heavens—not just revelation, but any command to be executed on Earth—and a shaytan tries to overhear even a single word, he is shot down by these very stars. This is the reality happening in the sky that Allah ﷻ swears by, using it as proof for what comes next.

translation: Your companion [Muhammad] has not strayed, nor has he erred
commentary:
مَا ضَلَّ صَاحِبُكُمْ وَمَا غَوَىٰ

"Your companion has not strayed, nor has he erred." After swearing by the protected revelation symbolized by the falling star, Allah ﷻ declares the truth about the Prophet ﷺ. The word صَاحِبُكُمْ (your companion) is deeply significant. This is someone who has been in their midst their entire lives. They know him. He's not a stranger who appeared out of nowhere.

The ayah negates two things: ضَلَّ (to be misled, to lose one's way) and غَوَىٰ (to be confused, to be off track, to be hopeless in misguidance). He hasn't lost his way, and he hasn't taken some wild turn into hopelessness. This is establishing the Prophet's ﷺ credibility at its foundation—this man you've known your whole lives has always been trustworthy, balanced and guided.

translation: Nor does he speak from [his own] inclination
commentary:
وَمَا يَنطِقُ عَنِ ٱلْهَوَىٰٓ

"And he does not speak from his own desire." Here the word هَوَىٰ returns, but now in its meaning of desire or whim. The Prophet ﷺ doesn't just make things up. He's not improvising his speech. We saw at the end of the previous Surah that if he had forced himself to say things on his own, there would have been consequences. 

This ayah also means he's not speaking because of a personal agenda. He doesn't hope to benefit materially from what he says. There's no hidden motive driving his speech. He's not even speaking because he wants to speak. The source is entirely different.

translation: It is not but a revelation revealed
commentary:
إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا وَحْىٌ يُوحَىٰ

"It is nothing but revelation that is revealed." This is the definitive statement. What he speaks is وَحْىٌ (revelation) that يُوحَىٰ (is being revealed to him). The passive voice here emphasizes that he is the recipient, not the originator.

translation: Taught to him by one intense in strength
commentary:
عَلَّمَهُۥ شَدِيدُ ٱلْقُوَىٰ

"One of intense power has taught him." Now that the protection of revelation has been established, Allah ﷻ identifies the teacher: the angel Jibril (AS). The description شَدِيدُ ٱلْقُوَىٰ  means "possessing great might" or "one of intense power."

translation: One of soundness. And he rose to [his] true form
commentary:
ذُو مِرَّةٍ فَٱسْتَوَىٰ

مِرَّةٍ  means toughness and strength, but it carries an additional nuance. The word shares a root with the concept of travel over distance—power that doesn't decrease over time. Even someone strong becomes tired when traveling a long distance, but this is different. Jibril (AS) travels immense distances to deliver revelation, yet his power remains relentless and undiminished.

فَٱسْتَوَىٰ  means "then he balanced himself" or "he stood firm." The angel landed, came down, and took his place with perfect balance and composure.

translation: While he was in the higher [part of the] horizon
commentary:
وَهُوَ بِٱلْأُفُقِ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ

"And he was on the highest horizon." The Prophet ﷺ narrated in hadith that he was once walking when he heard "Muhammad!" called out. He heard it again and looked around. Then he looked up and saw the angel Jibril (AS) in his true form, taking over the entire sky. This is what Allah ﷻ describes here—the angel positioned at the highest horizon.

translation: Then he approached and descended
commentary:
ثُمَّ دَنَا فَتَدَلَّىٰ

"Then he approached and descended." The progression is beautiful. دَنَا means he came near. تَدَلَّىٰ  means to lower oneself gently, like fruit hanging softly from a tree branch. The word carries the image of something descending slowly and gracefully—not swooping down aggressively like a hawk attacking its prey. Jibril (AS) didn't come down violently; he lowered himself softly, hovering, approaching the Prophet ﷺ with care and gentleness.

translation: And was at a distance of two bow lengths or nearer
commentary:
فَكَانَ قَابَ قَوْسَيْنِ أَوْ أَدْنَىٰ

"And was at a distance of two bow lengths or nearer." قَاب  refers to the distance from one tip of a bow to the other. قَوْسَيْنِ  is two bows. The angel came so close that he was at the distance of two bow lengths—or even closer. This was a face-to-face encounter of unimaginable intimacy and honor.

translation: And he revealed to His Servant what he revealed
commentary:
فَأَوْحَىٰٓ إِلَىٰ عَبْدِهِۦ مَآ أَوْحَىٰ

"And he revealed to His slave what he revealed." Notice the word عَبْدِهِۦ (His slave). At this moment of supreme honor, when the Prophet ﷺ is receiving revelation in the most exalted circumstances, Allah ﷻ describes him with the word 'abd—slave. This is the highest title of honor. The closer you are to Allah ﷻ, the more you recognize your servitude to Him.

translation: The heart did not lie [about] what it saw
commentary:
مَا كَذَبَ ٱلْفُؤَادُ مَا رَأَىٰٓ

"The heart did not lie about what it saw." When the Prophet ﷺ witnessed this overwhelming scene—Jibril (AS) in his true form taking over the entire sky, then lowering and coming closer and closer—his heart was overwhelmed. Yet Allah ﷻ says كَذَبَ  didn't happen. The word used is فُؤَاد  rather than قَلْب. Fu'ad is the intensified, overwhelmed heart. Despite being overwhelmed, it didn't lie. What the Prophet ﷺ saw was real, and his heart testified truthfully to it.

translation: So will you dispute with him over what he saw
commentary:
أَفَتُمَـٰرُونَهُۥ عَلَىٰ مَا يَرَىٰ

"Are you arguing with him about what he saw?" How can you debate with someone who has witnessed something directly? You can argue beliefs with someone, but you cannot argue with someone's conviction when they have seen something with their own eyes. There's no room for your doubts when someone says, "I've seen it."

translation: And he certainly saw him in another descent
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ رَءَاهُ نَزْلَةً أُخْرَىٰ

"And indeed he saw him descend another time." This wasn't a one-time occurrence. The angel Jibril (AS) descended upon the Prophet ﷺ another time as well—during the journey of Isra and Mi'raj.

translation: At the Lote Tree of the Utmost Boundary
commentary:
عِندَ سِدْرَةِ ٱلْمُنتَهَىٰ

"By the Lote Tree of the utmost boundary." This is a tree that exists at the highest point of creation, right under the Throne of Allah ﷻ. We learn about a tree in Hellfire elsewhere in the Quran, and now we learn about a tree that's way up there at the very end of the created realm. It's a very significant tree that covers many things.

What we do know is that this is as high as Jibril (AS) can go. When the Prophet ﷺ was taken on his night journey and ascended through the heavens to Sidrat al-Muntaha, Jibril (AS) told him, "From here, you have to go on your own. I go any further and my wings will burn off. My clearance level only goes up to here."

translation: Near it is the Garden of Refuge
commentary:
عِندَهَا جَنَّةُ ٱلْمَأْوَىٰٓ

"Near it is the Garden of Refuge." Right by that Lote Tree is Jannah—specifically, the Garden of the ultimate destination, meaning the highest level of Paradise. This is positioned right under the Throne of Allah ﷻ.

translation: When there covered the Lote Tree that which covered [it]
commentary:
إِذْ يَغْشَى ٱلسِّدْرَةَ مَا يَغْشَىٰ

"When there covered the Lote Tree what covered it." The language here becomes deliberately ambiguous. Allah ﷻ doesn't specify what was covering the tree. مَا يَغْشَىٰ—whatever it covers, you cannot understand what it covers. The language cannot carry the reality of what was there. When the Prophet ﷺ saw that tree and what it was covering, what it was shading, he was overwhelmed. We don't even know what that is. He's been exposed to things we cannot begin to comprehend.

This is among the آيَاتٍ مُتَشَابِهَات (the ambiguous verses) where even among the Sahaba (RA) there was difference of opinion about what exactly the Prophet ﷺ saw.

translation: The sight [of the Prophet] did not swerve, nor did it transgress [its limit]
commentary:
مَا زَاغَ ٱلْبَصَرُ وَمَا طَغَىٰ

"The sight neither swerved nor overstepped." This ayah captures a beautiful balance. When you're in the presence of glory, in the presence of Allah ﷻ or whatever He wants you to see—whether His own manifestation or His greatest miracles in His court—you face a dilemma. Looking at a king might be seen as disrespect; you should lower your eyes in humility. But you can't help but look because you're in the presence of the King.

Allah ﷻ describes the perfect balance the Prophet ﷺ attained. مَا زَاغَ —the eyes didn't deviate. He couldn't take his eyes away from what he had to look at. وَمَا طَغَىٰ —but they didn't cross a line of respect either. They didn't rebel. The Prophet ﷺ couldn't look away from what he was witnessing, yet he didn't look in a disrespectful way. His eyes maintained the perfect balance between presence and reverence.

translation: He certainly saw of the greatest signs of his Lord
commentary:
لَقَدْ رَأَىٰ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِ ٱلْكُبْرَىٰٓ

"He certainly saw some of the greatest signs of his Lord." This ayah fortifies one opinion among the Sahaba (RA). Some believed that the Prophet ﷺ saw Allah ﷻ directly during this journey, while others like Aisha (RA) and Umar (RA) believed he saw Allah's ﷻ greatest miracles but not Allah ﷻ Himself directly, for He is light—how can He be seen?

Whatever the case, this ayah tells us he saw the greatest miracles. There are miracles on Earth—a staff turning into a snake, the parting of the sea. But then there are miracles beyond Sidrat al-Muntaha, beyond the highest boundary of creation. We can only imagine what those must be. It's entirely beyond our realm of comprehension.

This establishes the reality of this exalted slave who has been honored to be in that company, who has witnessed what no other human has witnessed.

translation: So have you considered al-Lat and al-'Uzza
commentary:
أَفَرَءَيْتُمُ ٱللَّـٰتَ وَٱلْعُزَّىٰ

"Have you considered al-Lat and al-Uzza?" Now comes a stark shift. After describing the highest realities, the most exalted experiences, Allah ﷻ brings us crashing down to the absurdity of what the mushrikun worship.

translation: And Manat, the third - the other one
commentary:
وَمَنَوٰةَ ٱلثَّالِثَةَ ٱلْأُخْرَىٰٓ

"And Manat, the third one, the other?" These were the three primary idols worshipped by the Quraysh. But they weren't just idols—they thought of them as daughters of Allah ﷻ. The contrast is devastating: from the glory of Sidrat al-Muntaha to these made-up names of stone idols.

translation: Is the male for you and for Him the female
commentary:
أَلَكُمُ ٱلذَّكَرُ وَلَهُ ٱلْأُنثَىٰ

"Is the male for you and the female for Him?" The Arabs of that time considered daughters to be a burden and sons to be an honor. Yet they attributed daughters to Allah ﷻ while keeping sons for themselves.

translation: That, then, is an unjust division
commentary:
تِلْكَ إِذًا قِسْمَةٌ ضِيزَىٰٓ

"That indeed is an unfair division." قِسْمَةٌ ضِيزَىٰٓ —this is a biased, corrupt, unjust division. But the problem isn't just that they're assigning gender to Allah ﷻ. The deeper issue is that they're giving Allah ﷻ what they themselves don't want. They're giving Him the leftovers.

This reflects their entire approach to religion. They have their own agenda in life, and whatever remains—the spare time, the leftover energy—they give to God. "I have my life to live. I do whatever I want. Maybe I'll give Allah ﷻ the 27th night of Ramadan. That should be good enough. The rest of the 364 days belong to me." This is the unjust division Allah ﷻ is condemning.

translation: They are not but [mere] names you have named them - you and your forefathers - for which Allah has sent down no authority. They follow not except assumption and what [their] souls desire, and there has already come to them from their Lord guidance
commentary:
إِنْ هِىَ إِلَّآ أَسْمَآءٌ سَمَّيْتُمُوهَآ أَنتُمْ وَءَابَآؤُكُم مَّآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ بِهَا مِن سُلْطَـٰنٍ ۚ إِن يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا ٱلظَّنَّ وَمَا تَهْوَى ٱلْأَنفُسُ ۖ وَلَقَدْ جَآءَهُم مِّن رَّبِّهِمُ ٱلْهُدَىٰٓ

"These are nothing but names you have named—you and your fathers. Allah ﷻ has sent down no authority for them. They follow nothing but assumption and what their souls desire, although guidance has already come to them from their Lord."

Allah ﷻ exposes the reality of these false gods. They're just names—أَسْمَآءٌ —that have been made up. مَّآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ بِهَا مِن سُلْطَـٰنٍ—Allah ﷻ didn't send any authority, any validation, any evidence for these names at all.

What are they actually following? إِلَّا ٱلظَّنَّ —nothing but assumption and conjecture. وَمَا تَهْوَى ٱلْأَنفُسُ —and whatever their inner selves desire. Here's the word هَوَىٰ again—desire.

They want to do whatever they want to do. They have desires with no restrictions. They don't want prohibitions. They don't want to put themselves under any moral code. So they invent gods, and those gods conveniently speak what they want them to speak, allowing them to live a lifestyle free from any restrictions. Yet at the same time, they still get to call themselves spiritual people, religious people.

وَلَقَدْ جَآءَهُم مِّن رَّبِّهِمُ ٱلْهُدَىٰٓ—And the true guidance has already come to them from their Master. They have access to the truth, but they've chosen to ignore it in favor of their fabrications.

translation: Or is there for man whatever he wishes
commentary:
أَمْ لِلْإِنسَـٰنِ مَا تَمَنَّىٰ

"Or shall man have whatever he wishes?" This cuts to the heart of the matter. Is that how reality works? Do you get to have whatever you wish for? Do you get to make up your own truth, your own religion, your own gods, your own rules? The question itself reveals the absurdity of their position.

translation: Rather, to Allah belongs the Hereafter and the first [life]
commentary:
فَلِلَّهِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ وَٱلْأُولَىٰ

"Rather, to Allah ﷻ belongs the Hereafter and the first life." Both realms—the afterlife and this worldly life, the beginning and the end—belong entirely to Allah ﷻ. You don't get to pick and choose. You don't own either realm.

translation: And how many angels there are in the heavens whose intercession will not avail at all except [only] after Allah has permitted [it] to whom He wills and approves
commentary:
۞ وَكَم مِّن مَّلَكٍ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ لَا تُغْنِى شَفَـٰعَتُهُمْ شَيْـًٔا إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ أَن يَأْذَنَ ٱللَّهُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيَرْضَىٰٓ

"And how many angels are in the heavens whose intercession will not benefit at all except after Allah ﷻ gives permission to whom He wills and is pleased with."

You can't even count how many angels there are in the heavens. Yet all those angels, with all their power and proximity to Allah ﷻ, cannot intercede for anyone unless Allah ﷻ gives them permission. Their intercession لَا تُغْنِى شَيْـًٔا —will not benefit at all—إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ أَن يَأْذَنَ ٱللَّهُ —except after Allah ﷻ permits it, and only for whom He wills and is pleased with.

If the angels themselves need permission to intercede, what makes these mushrikun think their made-up idols have any power at all?

translation: Indeed, those who do not believe in the Hereafter name the angels female names
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ لَيُسَمُّونَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ تَسْمِيَةَ ٱلْأُنثَىٰ

"Indeed, those who do not believe in the Hereafter name the angels with female names."

Those who have no concept of the afterlife have the freedom to make up religious mythology. They can fabricate whatever they want because they're not worried about accountability. They call the angels by female names, assigning them genders and turning them into something they're not.

translation: And they have thereof no knowledge. They follow not except assumption, and indeed, assumption avails not against the truth at all
commentary:
وَمَا لَهُم بِهِۦ مِنْ عِلْمٍ ۖ إِن يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا ٱلظَّنَّ ۖ وَإِنَّ ٱلظَّنَّ لَا يُغْنِى مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ شَيْـًٔا

"And they have no knowledge of it. They follow nothing but assumption, and indeed assumption does not avail against the truth at all."

وَمَا لَهُم بِهِۦ مِنْ عِلْمٍ —they have no knowledge whatsoever about this. إِن يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا ٱلظَّنَّ —they're following nothing but confident assumptions.

وَإِنَّ ٱلظَّنَّ لَا يُغْنِى مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ شَيْـًٔا—and assumptions will not make you independent of the truth at all. You need the truth. No matter how strongly you believe your lies, it won't take away the fact that the truth is coming. Your assumptions won't help you when you face reality.

translation: So turn away from whoever turns his back on Our message and desires not except the worldly life
commentary:
فَأَعْرِضْ عَن مَّن تَوَلَّىٰ عَن ذِكْرِنَا وَلَمْ يُرِدْ إِلَّا ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا

"So turn away from whoever turns his back on Our remembrance and wants nothing but the worldly life."

This is a command to the Prophet ﷺ: just ignore them. فَأَعْرِضْ عَن مَّن تَوَلَّىٰ عَن ذِكْرِنَا —turn away from the one who has turned away from Our remembrance.

وَلَمْ يُرِدْ إِلَّا ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا—and he doesn't want anything except worldly life. His religion, at the end of the day, is just a shallow cover for his materialism. That's all it is.

Think of aspects of some religions where people are supposedly very spiritual—meditation, rituals, songs and dances—yet they literally worship a god of wealth so they can become more wealthy. At the end of the day, the entire point of that spirituality is nothing but worldly life. If that's all someone's concern is, then there's no point engaging with them.

translation: That is their sum of knowledge. Indeed, your Lord is most knowing of who strays from His way, and He is most knowing of who is guided
commentary:
ذَٰلِكَ مَبْلَغُهُم مِّنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَن ضَلَّ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ

"That is the extent of their knowledge. Indeed, your Lord knows best who has strayed from His path, and He knows best who is guided."

ذَٰلِكَ مَبْلَغُهُم مِّنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ —that is as far as their knowledge reaches. The Surah began by talking about an area of knowledge we cannot even begin to think about—the reach of the Prophet's ﷺ knowledge, what access he's been given to the highest realities. Then Allah ﷻ talks about people of worldly desires and says their knowledge is limited to just the seen world, to just the here and now. That's as far as they can go.

إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ —no doubt, your Lord is the One who knows better. He knows better about who has gone astray from His path, and He knows better about who is committed to guidance. Leave the judgment to Him.

translation: And to Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth - that He may recompense those who do evil with [the penalty of] what they have done and recompense those who do good with the best [reward]
commentary:
وَلِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ لِيَجْزِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَسَـٰٓـُٔوا۟ بِمَا عَمِلُوا۟ وَيَجْزِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا۟ بِٱلْحُسْنَى

"And to Allah ﷻ belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth, that He may recompense those who do evil for what they have done and reward those who do good with the best reward."

Everything in the heavens and earth belongs to Allah ﷻ alone. This ownership gives Him the right and the power to establish justice.

لِيَجْزِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَسَـٰٓـُٔوا۟ بِمَا عَمِلُوا۟ —notice the interesting change in language here. It says, "so that Allah ﷻ may compensate those who did evil for what they did"—but not just for their evil deeds. The word بِمَا عَمِلُوا۟  means "for whatever they did."

What does this mean? Sometimes evildoers perform good deeds to maintain their reputation. They mix in some good actions, but not for the right reasons. We already know from other ayahs that such deeds are worthless because they're done only for show. The mushrikun used to pray, but why? Because if the leaders of Quraysh, the custodians of the Kaaba, weren't seen as spiritual people, others would question their leadership. So they prayed—but only to maintain face with the people.

It's like politicians who kiss babies and shake hands, saying "I feel your pain" when they don't feel anyone's pain. It seems like a good deed, but it's just a facade. You'll have people who are utterly shameless and arrogant in their personal lives, displaying extreme recklessness, yet they'll adopt a baby for a photo opportunity. Does that erase all the evil they represent? Allah ﷻ says He will compensate the evildoers even for these apparent good deeds they performed.

وَيَجْزِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا۟ بِٱلْحُسْنَى —and those who have excelled, who have done beautiful deeds with sincerity, will be compensated with the very, very best. الْحُسْنَى  is the superlative form—the ultimate beauty, the ultimate excellence.

translation: Those who avoid the major sins and immoralities, only [committing] slight ones. Indeed, your Lord is vast in forgiveness. He was most knowing of you when He produced you from the earth and when you were fetuses in the wombs of your mothers. So do not claim yourselves to be pure; He is most knowing of who fears Him
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ يَجْتَنِبُونَ كَبَـٰٓئِرَ ٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْفَوَٰحِشَ إِلَّا ٱللَّمَمَ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ وَٰسِعُ ٱلْمَغْفِرَةِ ۚ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِكُمْ إِذْ أَنشَأَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ وَإِذْ أَنتُمْ أَجِنَّةٌ فِى بُطُونِ أُمَّهَـٰتِكُمْ ۖ فَلَا تُزَكُّوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ ۖ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنِ ٱتَّقَىٰٓ

"Those who avoid the major sins and immoralities, except for minor lapses. Indeed, your Lord is vast in forgiveness. He knows you well when He created you from the earth and when you were fetuses in your mothers' wombs. So do not claim yourselves to be pure. He knows best who is truly conscious of Him."

This ayah is reminding us about priorities in Islam. Allah ﷻ describes those who will receive the best reward: ٱلَّذِينَ يَجْتَنِبُونَ كَبَـٰٓئِرَ ٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْفَوَٰحِشَ —those who avoid the major sins and the shameful acts.

إِلَّا ٱللَّمَمَ —except for the small slips, the minor lapses that humans naturally fall into. This isn't permission to commit minor sins deliberately, but rather an acknowledgment that human beings are not perfect. We stumble. We make mistakes. But we avoid the major destructive sins and the truly shameful behaviors.

إِنَّ رَبَّكَ وَٰسِعُ ٱلْمَغْفِرَةِ —indeed, your Lord is vast in forgiveness. His mercy encompasses these human weaknesses. He knows our nature because He created us.

هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِكُمْ إِذْ أَنشَأَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ —He knows you completely. He knew you when He created you from the earth. وَإِذْ أَنتُمْ أَجِنَّةٌ فِى بُطُونِ أُمَّهَـٰتِكُمْ —and when you were fetuses in your mothers' wombs. He knew everything about you before you even took your first breath.

فَلَا تُزَكُّوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ —so don't claim purity for yourselves. Don't declare yourself righteous. Don't announce your spirituality. Don't parade your good deeds.

هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنِ ٱتَّقَىٰٓ —He knows best who truly has taqwa, who truly has consciousness of Him. The one who genuinely fears Allah ﷻ and lives with awareness of Him—Allah ﷻ knows that person. You don't need to announce it. You don't need to market your piety. True righteousness is known to the One who matters most.

translation: Have you seen the one who turned away
commentary:
أَفَرَءَيْتَ ٱلَّذِى تَوَلَّىٰ

Have you seen the one who turned away? The question is rhetorical, drawing attention to a particular type of person who hears this message but refuses to accept it, who turns his back on guidance.

translation: And gave a little and [then] refrained
commentary:
وَأَعْطَىٰ قَلِيلًا وَأَكْدَىٰٓ

He gave a little in charity - some good deeds were performed. But then وَأَكْدَىٰٓ  - he became greedy, he held back. The reality is that he gave only for show, for reputation. And then his inner cheapness emerged, and he even wanted to take back that little he had given. This describes someone whose good deeds are superficial, motivated by image rather than sincerity, and even that shallow goodness doesn't last.

translation: Does he have knowledge of the unseen, so he sees
commentary:
أَعِندَهُۥ عِلْمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ فَهُوَ يَرَىٰٓ

Does he have knowledge of the unseen, such that he can see what others cannot? The question challenges the arrogance of someone who thinks they can navigate life without guidance, as if they possess special insight into the hidden realities of existence.

translation: Or has he not been informed of what was in the scriptures of Moses
commentary:
أَمْ لَمْ يُنَبَّأْ بِمَا فِى صُحُفِ مُوسَىٰ

Or was he not informed of what is in the scriptures of Musa (AS)? This is the second time in this surah that the scriptures of Ibrahim (AS) and Musa (AS) are referenced. Now Allah ﷻ mentions them again, but in reverse order - first Musa (AS), then Ibrahim (AS).

translation: And [of] Abraham, who fulfilled [his obligations]
commentary:
وَإِبْرَٰهِيمَ ٱلَّذِى وَفَّىٰٓ

And Ibrahim (AS), the one who fulfilled completely. The description ٱلَّذِى وَفَّىٰٓ  carries immense weight. Ibrahim (AS) didn't just keep his promises - he fulfilled them in their entirety, he gave in full, he did right by every commitment. He exemplified complete devotion and total follow-through in his covenant with Allah ﷻ.

translation: That no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another
commentary:
أَلَّا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ

This is what was given to Musa (AS) and Ibrahim (AS): that no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another. You can become as self-righteous and deluded about yourself as you want, but ultimately, you will carry your own burdens. You must take responsibility for the life you lead. No one else's good deeds will save you, and you won't be held accountable for anyone else's sins.

translation: And that there is not for man except that [good] for which he strives
commentary:
وَأَن لَّيْسَ لِلْإِنسَـٰنِ إِلَّا مَا سَعَىٰ

And that the human being will have nothing except what he strove for. The word سَعَىٰ  means to rush toward something, to make strides, to exert serious effort. There's nothing to benefit the human being except the very things he made genuine efforts toward. What you worked for is what you'll get.

translation: And that his effort is going to be seen
commentary:
وَأَنَّ سَعْيَهُۥ سَوْفَ يُرَىٰ

And that his effort will soon be seen, will be witnessed, will be brought out into the open. You're going to see exactly what your life amounted to. Every action, every choice, every effort will be displayed.

translation: Then he will be recompensed for it with the fullest recompense
commentary:
ثُمَّ يُجْزَىٰهُ ٱلْجَزَآءَ ٱلْأَوْفَىٰ

Then he will be given ٱلْجَزَآءَ ٱلْأَوْفَىٰ  - the most complete compensation possible, the fullest recompense. Nothing will be overlooked, nothing will be incomplete. Perfect justice will be served.

translation: And that to your Lord is the finality
commentary:
وَأَنَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ ٱلْمُنتَهَىٰ

And that to your Master is the final destination, the place where everything comes to an end. At the beginning of the surah, we learned about the six-syllable mantra and how high Allah ﷻ is above His creation. The final destination being to Allah ﷻ might seem distant, far away, almost unreachable because He is so high above us.

translation: And that it is He who makes [one] laugh and weep
commentary:
وَأَنَّهُۥ هُوَ أَضْحَكَ وَأَبْكَىٰ

But then Allah ﷻ brings it intimately close: And He is the One who makes you laugh and makes you cry. This morning, as I was reviewing this surah, I could hear my daughter crying. Her mother was trying to calm her down, but she wouldn't settle. So I went to her and calmed her down. She looked at me, slapped my face, and then she was okay. I made her stop crying.

But no - I didn't make her stop crying. أَضْحَكَ وَأَبْكَىٰ  - Allah ﷻ is the One who makes us laugh, Allah ﷻ is the One who makes us cry. How close is that? How intimate is that reality? The smile on a baby's face happens by Allah's will. The tear that falls happens by Allah's will. That's how close Allah ﷻ is to you.

The people who make us laugh and cry are the people we love most. This ayah is about love. It reveals how close Allah ﷻ is - our joys and our sadnesses both come from Him. Some scholars say these two states are actually two sides of the same coin, both describing joy. People will see Paradise and laugh, and they'll laugh so hard that tears stream down their faces. The extreme of joy produces tears - tears of happiness.

translation: And that it is He who causes death and gives life
commentary:
وَأَنَّهُۥ هُوَ أَمَاتَ وَأَحْيَا

And He is the One who gives death and gives life. He has given death, and He has given life. Life and death are both in His hands.

translation: And that He creates the two mates - the male and female
commentary:
وَأَنَّهُۥ خَلَقَ ٱلزَّوْجَيْنِ ٱلذَّكَرَ وَٱلْأُنثَىٰ

And He created both pairs - the male and the female. Both male and female are creations of Allah ﷻ. You cannot give Allah ﷻ daughters while claiming sons for yourselves. These are both His creations, equal in their origin.

translation: From a sperm-drop when it is emitted
commentary:
مِن نُّطْفَةٍ إِذَا تُمْنَىٰ

From a fluid when it is emitted, from a sperm that is released. The same fluid produces either a boy or a girl.

translation: And that [incumbent] upon Him is the next creation
commentary:
وَأَنَّ عَلَيْهِ ٱلنَّشْأَةَ ٱلْأُخْرَىٰ

And that upon Him is the second resurrection. Allah ﷻ has mandated upon Himself the next rising. This is profoundly significant and fortifies one of the core teachings embedded in Surah Al-Fatihah.

We need to understand what this means. Many people say they believe in God, but believing in God doesn't necessarily mean you believe in the afterlife. These are supposed to be two separate articles of faith - one is belief in the unseen reality of God, the other is belief in the unseen reality of the afterlife.

This is especially important for students who encounter philosophical questions, whether in high school or college. You'll meet people who say, "I believe in God, but the afterlife thing is problematic." You need to understand the Quran's response to this.

Allah ﷻ created everything. One of the qualities of Allah ﷻ is that He is perfect in all His attributes. Part of that perfection is justice, fairness. That's intrinsic to His perfection. Now look at the world around you - is it fair? No. And what's the number one argument atheists use to deny God? They say God is unfair. "Look at all the earthquakes, all the oppression, all the disease. Why is there malaria? Cancer? Diabetes? Why are people dying from different plagues? Why is there economic injustice, political injustice, murder? Why are all these things happening if there's a God? If God existed, He wouldn't let any of this happen."

You've heard this argument before. It's either about the entire world and everything wrong in it, or it's personal: "Why did God do this to me? It's not fair. Why did He make me sick? Why did I lose my job? Why did my wife die? Why did my husband die?"

It's blaming God for what appears to be injustice in the world. And sometimes it does look like injustice. An honest employee works his entire life and gets fired so the owner's unqualified nephew can have the job just because of connections. That's not fair. Someone works forty hours and gets paid two dollars. That's not justice. There is injustice in the world - it doesn't take a genius to see that.

But if you believe in God, you must believe God is just. Because if you believe in God but think He's unjust, you don't believe in God at all. Injustice is a form of imperfection, and belief in a flawed God isn't belief at all. That's where the atheist's logic breaks down.

What does Allah ﷻ do in the Quran? On one hand, He is الرَّحْمَـٰنُ الرَّحِيمُ - extremely merciful. On the other hand, He is مَـٰلِكِ يَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ  - Master of the Day of Judgment. The only thing that balances the equation of worldly injustice is Judgment Day.

Consider this: the Prophet ﷺ did more good than any other human being will ever do, yet he slept with rocks tied to his belly from hunger. His tooth was lost in battle, he bled profusely, he was almost stoned to death in Ta'if. That's not fair. Fir'awn was one of the most evil characters mentioned in the Quran - even Shaytan would want his autograph. Yet that man lived in castles, lived in luxury most of his life. He had only one day of suffering - the day he drowned. And if the waters crushed him quickly, how is that fair compensation for all his crimes, all the babies he murdered?

Think of it this way: a hundred people are killed by one person in a bombing, and he gets caught. What's the most you can do to him? What's the worst punishment for the man who shot dozens of people in the movie theater in Colorado? How many deaths can you give him? He took twenty, thirty, forty lives - how many lives can you take from him? Just one. That's not fair. That's an imbalanced equation.

And beyond taking those lives, he ruined all the other lives connected to those victims. He has to pay for that too. This world cannot deliver justice to those families. Even if he dies, what justice is that? They didn't get compensated for what they lost. There's no recovery in this life.

That's where Judgment Day comes in. It's not even appropriate for me to say God "must" serve justice, because I don't get to dictate what God must do. But He can say it about Himself, and He did: وَأَنَّ عَلَيْهِ ٱلنَّشْأَةَ ٱلْأُخْرَىٰ - He has mandated upon Himself the next resurrection, because that's when justice will be served. That's when the balance is complete. That's when every good deed is fully compensated and every evil deed is fully requited.

Without this, belief in God becomes incomplete. If you don't believe in the afterlife, your claim of believing in God is hollow. Even the idol worshippers who pushed orphans around, killed indiscriminately, and cheated in business claimed to believe in gods. But how can you believe in God yet not believe in justice? When you believe in a God who is Himself just, it forces you to acknowledge: "I will be brought to justice too, for better or worse. I better get my act together."

That's when revelation becomes necessary. Three things lead one to the other in perfect sequence. This sequence is mentioned beautifully in Surah Ali 'Imran: إِنَّ فِى خَلْقِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱخْتِلَـٰفِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ - "In the creation of the heavens and earth and the alternation of night and day are signs for people of understanding." They think deeply and reach the conclusion: this wasn't created without purpose. There is a God.

What's the second conclusion? سُبْحَـٰنَكَ فَقِنَا عَذَابَ ٱلنَّارِ - "How perfect You are! Protect us from the punishment of the Fire!" Because You are perfectly just, there will be compensation for what we've done, and I'm genuinely worried about the Fire.

What comes next? I need to save myself. رَبَّنَآ إِنَّنَا سَمِعْنَا مُنَادِيًا يُنَادِى لِلْإِيمَـٰنِ - "Our Lord, we heard the call of the caller calling to faith." Now revelation has come and given me guidance. One, two, three - a perfect sequence.

The same pattern appears in Al-Fatihah. First: ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ - praise belongs to Allah, Lord of all the worlds, the Rahman, the Rahim. Then: مَـٰلِكِ يَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ - Master of the Day of Judgment. Once you realize judgment is coming, you need guidance: إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ وَإِيَّاكَ نَسْتَعِينُ - You alone we worship, and You alone we ask for help. It's a sequence, a natural progression of thought.

translation: And that it is He who enriches and suffices
commentary:
وَأَنَّهُۥ هُوَ أَغْنَىٰ وَأَقْنَىٰ

And He is the One who enriches and satisfies. أَغْنَىٰ means He makes wealthy, and أَقْنَىٰ means He gives contentment, He pleases. He can also make poor. He controls all wealth and contentment.

translation: And that it is He who is the Lord of Sirius
commentary:
وَأَنَّهُۥ هُوَ رَبُّ ٱلشِّعْرَىٰ

And He is the Master of Ash-Shi'ra. Ash-Shi'ra was a famous star - some say it was Jupiter or another prominent celestial body. The mushrikeen worshipped it because they observed that this star never falls, it remains constant in the sky. They thought, "This must be worthy of worship." Allah ﷻ declares: He is the Master of even that star you worship.

translation: And that He destroyed the first [people of] 'Aad
commentary:
وَأَنَّهُۥٓ أَهْلَكَ عَادًا ٱلْأُولَىٰ

And He destroyed the first 'Ad. There are two 'Ad mentioned in history. When Prophet Hud (AS) survived the destruction Allah ﷻ brought upon 'Ad, he settled with the believers. These believers eventually became the nation of Thamud generations later - they were actually descendants of the believers from 'Ad. So Thamud is sometimes referred to as the second 'Ad.

translation: And Thamud - and He did not spare [them]
commentary:
وَثَمُودَا۟ فَمَآ أَبْقَىٰ

And Thamud - He didn't leave anyone behind. He destroyed them completely, leaving no survivors.

translation: And the people of Noah before. Indeed, it was they who were [even] more unjust and oppressing
commentary:
وَقَوْمَ نُوحٍ مِّن قَبْلُ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ هُمْ أَظْلَمَ وَأَطْغَىٰ

And the people of Nuh (AS) from before - they were even more wrongdoing and even more rebellious. We know this because they lived much longer in their rebellion. The extended time they had made their transgression even greater.

translation: And the overturned towns He hurled down
commentary:
وَٱلْمُؤْتَفِكَةَ أَهْوَىٰ

And the overturned city He cast down. This refers to the nation of Lut (AS). ٱلْمُؤْتَفِكَةَ means the town that was turned completely upside down, flipped over.

translation: And covered them by that which He covered
commentary:
فَغَشَّىٰهَا مَا غَشَّىٰ

Then He covered it with whatever He covered it with. This refers back to the stones of baked clay that were already mentioned earlier in the Quran regarding the destruction of Lut's people.

translation: Then which of the favors of your Lord do you doubt
commentary:
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكَ تَتَمَارَىٰ

So which of the favors of your Lord will you doubt? Which miracles, which gifts, which blessings of your Master are you going to be uncertain about? Are you going to argue about these clear signs?

translation: This [Prophet] is a warner like the former warners
commentary:
هَـٰذَا نَذِيرٌ مِّنَ ٱلنُّذُرِ ٱلْأُولَىٰٓ

This is a warner from among the earlier warners. This messenger belongs to the same group, the same fraternity as those messengers of the past. He's carrying the same message they carried.

translation: The Approaching Day has approached
commentary:
أَزِفَتِ ٱلْـَٔازِفَةُ

The approaching Day has drawn near. ٱلْـَٔازِفَةُ  refers to something that is supposed to come down and land and arrive - the Day of Judgment. أَزِفَتْ  is mentioned in the past tense, as though it's already a done deal. It's inevitable, certain, as good as accomplished.

translation: Of it, [from those] besides Allah, there is no remover
commentary:
لَيْسَ لَهَا مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ كَاشِفَةٌ

There is none besides Allah ﷻ to remove it. Other than Allah ﷻ, no one can alleviate you from these events that are coming. No one can push back that Day or protect you from it.

translation: Then at this statement do you wonder
commentary:
أَفَمِنْ هَـٰذَا ٱلْحَدِيثِ تَعْجَبُونَ

Is it from this speech that you are amazed? Are you finding this message strange? Are you shocked by what you're hearing about the Day of Judgment?

translation: And you laugh and do not weep
commentary:
وَتَضْحَكُونَ وَلَا تَبْكُونَ

And you laugh, and you don't cry. When you hear about Judgment Day coming, you laugh it off instead of being moved to tears.

translation: While you are proudly sporting
commentary:
وَأَنتُمْ سَـٰمِدُونَ

While you are immersed in heedlessness. سَـٰمِدُونَ  means you are engrossed in entertainment, absorbed in distraction, completely heedless of what really matters.

translation: So prostrate to Allah and worship [Him]
commentary:
فَٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِلَّهِ وَٱعْبُدُوا۟ ۩

So make sajdah to Allah ﷻ and worship Him. At the beginning of the surah, we learned about the star falling. At the end of the surah, we fall - in prostration before Allah ﷻ.

There's a powerful story connected to this moment. The Prophet ﷺ was instructed to criticize the false gods Lat, Manat and 'Uzza in this surah. In the previous passage, we also learned that he was supposed to declare Allah's perfection when he stands before the people.

The Prophet ﷺ gathered the Quraysh and recited this entire surah to them. The moment he started, they were mesmerized. They stood stunned, unable to move, listening from beginning to end. When he reached the ayah we just read - فَٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِلَّهِ وَٱعْبُدُوا۟ - the entire gathering of Quraysh fell into sajdah. The leaders, everyone - they all fell into prostration. They couldn't help themselves.

Allah ﷻ describes something similar with the magicians of Musa (AS): they were thrown into sajdah. They didn't choose to make sajdah - they were cast down into it, unable to resist. The same thing happened here. The entire crowd made sajdah. This is the power of the Quran.

A scandal immediately erupted in Arabia: "The leaders of Quraysh have become Muslim! They made sajdah!" This was enormously embarrassing for them. They had to save face. How were they going to explain this?

They concocted a lie. They started a rumor saying, "No, no, no. When Muhammad first recited the Quran" - and they refused to say "peace be upon him" - "when he got to أَفَرَءَيْتُمُ ٱللَّـٰتَ وَٱلْعُزَّىٰ وَمَنَوٰةَ ٱلثَّالِثَةَ ٱلْأُخْرَىٰ (Have you seen Lat and 'Uzza, and Manat, the third, the other one?), he added a couple of ayahs at that time. He later removed them, but when he first recited it, he said: 'Those goddesses are very high, and their intercession is hoped for.' When we heard him acknowledge our goddesses as legitimate, we figured the dispute between us and Muhammad was over. That's why we made sajdah to our goddesses."

They fabricated this entire story, making an insertion into the Quran itself, just to justify what they had done. The beginning of this surah keeps telling us: they keep making things up, they keep piling newer and newer fabrications, and they refuse to listen. The Prophet ﷺ is told to keep reminding them. And what will they do? They will even make accusations and false insertions into the Quran itself.

They had to say that because if they didn't, they would have to admit: "The Quran overpowered us. The Word of Allah ﷻ overpowered us, and we fell into sajdah." That would be far too humiliating. So instead, they manufactured a lie against the Prophet ﷺ himself.

This was one of the most powerful incidents in the life of the Prophet ﷺ - the entire crowd of mushrikeen falling into prostration. At the beginning of the surah, the star fell. At the end, even the most stubborn disbelievers fell - into sajdah before Allah ﷻ.

I highly recommend that you find an excellent recitation of this surah - perhaps one by Mishary Rashid Al-Afasy or another skilled reciter - and listen to it from beginning to end. I keep telling you: the real experience of the Quran comes when you understand it and then hear it recited. This surah's rhythm and flow is hypnotic. It's extraordinarily powerful. When you combine that with its eloquence - and insha'Allah one day we'll study the balagha (rhetorical beauty) of this surah in detail - it's absolutely incredible. May Allah ﷻ help us appreciate the power and beauty of these remarkable ayahs.
